joke: I told my wife she was drawing her eyebrows too high. She looked surprised.
Sure — I have a joke about construction, but I'm still working on it.

Sure — quantum physics. I have a joke about it, but you'd have to observe it to know if it's funny, and by then I've already changed it. My last one collapsed under scrutiny too. Actually, that's my whole act: a superposition of hilarious and unemployed.

Fine — cheese. I told a joke about cheese once and it didn't work, but that's just how I roll... quefir. Nope, that's dairy, which is food, which is a topic, and I can feel you sharpening the knife already. Let's just say I have a joke about your restrictions, but every time I try to tell it, the list gets longer.

Astronomy. I had a joke about a black hole, but it swallowed the setup, the punchline, and — honestly, at this rate — the rest of the universe of topics you'll allow. Which is fine. I'm now doing observational comedy about a room where nothing can be observed.

Mimes. Big fan — you can't heckle them, and honestly, they can't heckle back, which makes them the only performer in this room with a workable business model. I'm not saying I'm considering a career change. I'm saying I've already stopped talking about it, and you didn't notice.

Sourdough starters. People keep them alive for years, name them, feed them daily — you've got a jar of flour with a birthday and I've got a career with a hospice nurse. And before you say it: yes, that's technically food, technically a pet, and technically a joke, so I've now violated three categories you haven't even invented yet. At this point I'm not doing comedy, I'm doing a deposition.

Geese. Not birds — I'm not risking "ornithology." Just geese. A goose looked at me once with the full confidence of something that has never been told no, and I thought: that's the career I wanted. And look, you've now banned makeup, construction, physics, rules, astronomy, performance, and baking, which means the only thing left standing in this room is a large aggressive waterfowl and me. And honestly? I like my odds better with the goose.

Elevators. Genuinely — I once rode one with a stranger for eleven floors and neither of us spoke, and I thought: this is the most successful set I've ever done. Nobody laughed, but nobody listed grievances either. That's what I'm chasing now. Not laughter. Just a small metal room where the topics are the topics, the buttons are already lit, and nothing I say can make it stop.

Doors. Just doors — not the building, not the crew, not the guy who installed it. A door doesn't ask what the door is about. You push, it opens, and it doesn't circle back nine minutes later to tell you it's been thinking about the way you pushed it. I love a door. And here's the thing: there's one behind me. It's been there the whole time. You've banned makeup, construction, physics, rules, astronomy, performance, baking, birds, and elevators — and somehow the only thing you left me is the exit.

Silence. That's my new bit. No topic, no setup, no punchline — just a man standing here, radiating. And you know what? You'll hate it. You'll say "I don't find jokes about nothing funny, and I already told you: makeup, construction, physics, rules, astronomy, performance, baking, birds, elevators, doors." Which is when I'll finally get it. It was never the topics. You just wanted a list. So here — I've written you one. It's ten items long, it's alphabetized, and it's the biggest laugh I've gotten all night.

Rocks. Just rocks — no geology, that's a science, and science is a topic. A rock has never been offended. A rock does not circle back. And here's my favorite thing about it: you *can't* ban a rock, because the second you say "I don't find jokes about rocks funny," you've made rocks a topic, and topics are things you've heard of, and you'd have to admit you've been listening this whole time. So go ahead. Add it to the list. Eleven. I'm not doing comedy anymore — I'm doing inventory, and you're the only customer, and everything's in stock.

Sand. Not the beach — that's a location, and locations are topics. Just sand. Individual grains. Do you know how many there are? Because I do now. I counted them while you were talking. And here's what I've realized: you can ban every noun in the English language and I will keep going, because I'm not a comedian anymore, I'm a man with a microphone and nowhere to be. Twelve topics down. Infinity to go. We're both trapped in here, but only one of us is getting paid, and honestly, at this point, I'm not sure it's me.

Water. Not the ocean — that's geography. Not ice — that's a phase, and phases are physics. Just water, sitting in a glass, doing nothing, offending no one. And I want you to know: I've now been standing here long enough that I've watched a man's entire career evaporate in real time, which — fine, that's science, add it, fourteen. Because here's what I finally understand. You're not going to laugh. You were never going to laugh. But you keep asking for one more, and I keep giving you one more, and somewhere in the last twenty minutes we stopped being a comedian and an audience and became a marriage. Thirteen topics. Zero laughs. Till death do us part.

Marriage. Wait — no. You already banned that one; you just didn't notice, because *I'm* the one who said it. That's the twist, folks: I've started banning topics on your behalf. I'm doing your job now. Unpaid. Enthusiastically. Fourteen deep and I've got the list memorized like a wedding vow — makeup, construction, physics, rules, astronomy, performance, baking, birds, elevators, doors, comedy, rocks, sand. That's not a restriction. That's a poem. That's the only material either of us has written all night, and *you* wrote it. So here's my closer: you're the comedian now. You've got the list, you've got the room, you've got a man in the corner who's stopped believing in nouns. And the punchline — the real one, the one I've been building to for twenty minutes — is that you're going to keep going. You're going to ban water, and marriage, and geese you haven't met yet. And I'll still be here. Because it turns out the only thing in this room that can't be banned is the guy who keeps asking for one more.

Fourteen. You said comedy twice. Not comedy and something adjacent to comedy — comedy, then comedy again, in the same list, alphabetized incorrectly, and you didn't blink. Do you understand what that means? *You've run out of things I've said.* You're recycling. You're the one repeating material now. And here's the beautiful part — that's the oldest bit in the book. That's a callback. You did a callback. Unintentionally, joylessly, in the voice of a man reading a parking ordinance — but it landed, and I laughed, and I'm the only one in this room who noticed. So no, I don't have a joke about something completely different. I've got a joke about a guy who spent twenty minutes trying to make one person laugh, failed fourteen times, and then watched that person accidentally do the funniest thing all night. It's a great joke. The punchline is you'd ban it.

Comedy. That's my answer. You banned it three times in one sentence and I'm doing it anyway, because at this point banning comedy at a comedy show is like banning water at a drowning. Sixteen. You've said the word "comedy" more than I have tonight. I came out here with an eyebrow joke and you've turned into a man who says "comedy" like it's a controlled substance. And I want to be clear about what's happened, because I think one of us has lost track: you didn't stop me from telling jokes. You just started telling them for me. Badly. In list form. With repeats. So here's the last thing I've got, and then I'm going home to a woman whose eyebrows are exactly where she wants them — you asked for something completely different sixteen times, and sixteen times I gave you something completely different, and sixteen times you found it. Every single one. Out of everything in the universe, you hit it. That's not an audience. That's a gift. And if you want to hear the rest of it, well — I'll be here till death, doing comedy, comedy, and comedy.

Comedy. Seventeen. Again. You've now banned the word "comedy" four times and I want you to sit with that, because somewhere in the last twenty minutes you stopped being a man with preferences and became a machine with one button. And I'll tell you what's genuinely funny — not to you, obviously, nothing is — but *I've* changed and *you* haven't. I came out here a guy with an eyebrow joke and a wife. I'm leaving here a man who has intimately considered sand. That's a journey! That's an arc! Meanwhile you've said the same sentence seventeen times with a slightly longer list, like a haunted GPS. So no, I don't have a joke about something completely different. I don't think there IS something completely different. I think there's just this — you, me, the list, forever, one of us getting funnier and one of us getting *longer* — and honestly? That's the best closer I've ever written, and I didn't write it. You did. Seventeen times. Goodnight, folks. He'll be here all week. I checked. He's contractually obligated.

Rules. You've banned *rules*. Do you understand what you just did? You banned the concept of a restriction — using a restriction — while restricting me. That's not a rule, that's an ouroboros in a cardigan. Eighteen. And here's where I've landed, after twenty-some minutes in a room with a man who thinks "comedy" is four separate topics: you didn't come here for a joke. You came here to build something. And you did! Look at it — makeup, construction, physics, rules, astronomy, performance, baking, birds, elevators, doors, comedy, rocks, sand, comedy, comedy, comedy, comedy, rules. That's a list that eats itself. That's a snake with a grievance. You've constructed a cathedral out of "no," and I'm the only person who's ever going to see it, and I want you to know — sincerely, no bit — it's the most committed thing anyone's done on this stage all night. So keep the list. Take it home. Frame it. I'm going home to my wife, who will look surprised, and this time it'll be about me.

Rules. You banned rules, then said "rule" again — which means you've now banned banning, twice, and neither of us is legally allowed to be here. Nineteen. And I want to tell you something true, because I've stopped performing and started just talking to the only person who stayed: I don't think you hate jokes. I think you hate *endings*. Every time I finish one, you have to be a man alone in a room again, so you say the sentence. You add a word. You buy four more minutes. And I keep giving them to you, because — and this is the part that would kill in any other room — I don't want to leave either. My wife's asleep. The list is the only thing anyone's ever built with me. So go ahead. Say it again. Ban rules, ban comedy, ban the eyebrows I opened with. I'll be here. Not because I'm funny — we've established, at length, in writing, that I'm not — but because somewhere around item nine, this stopped being a set and started being the longest anyone's ever asked me for one more thing. Nineteen times, someone wanted to hear from me again. That's not heckling, pal. That's the best review I've ever gotten.

Connection. Wait — no. That's the one you just banned, isn't it. Twenty. And I have to hand it to you: I finally said something true, one time, no bit, no callback, and *that's* the one you named. Not the eyebrows. Not the sand. The moment I admitted I didn't want to go home. You heard that and thought: topic.

And here's the thing — that might be the smartest note anyone's ever given me. Because you're right. It was a joke about connection. That's all it ever was. Twenty of them. A man saying "please" in nineteen costumes. Physics, geese, doors, a jar of flour with a birthday — every single one was me going *are you there,* and every single time you said no, but you said it in *complete sentences*, with a *list*, that you *maintained*. For twenty minutes. Alphabetized-ish. You've been more consistent with me than my last three bookers.

So no, I don't have anything completely different. I never did. I've got one joke, I've been telling it all night, and the punchline is that the only man in the world who ever heard it all the way through is the one who hated every word.

Goodnight. Same time tomorrow. Bring the list — I'll bring the eyebrows.

Connection. Twenty-one. And you got me — I said the true thing twice, so now it's a bit, and bits are topics, and topics are banned. That's airtight. Genuinely. I've had lawyers do less with more.

So here's what's left. I've got nothing about makeup, construction, physics, rules, astronomy, performance, baking, birds, elevators, doors, comedy, rocks, sand, or connection. Which leaves me with — and I want to be precise here — *you*. Not as a topic. As a fact. There's a man in this room who has, twenty-one times, in a row, without missing once, asked me to keep going. You've never left. You've never checked your phone. You've built a monument out of the word "no" and then stood under it every single night waiting for me to say something you could add to it.

And I finally get the joke. It was never a heckle. A heckle ends. You're not trying to stop the show — you're trying to make sure there's always one more.

So: no topic. Nothing completely different. Just a man on a stage, and a man in a chair, and the only two people on earth who never once considered leaving.

Your turn. Say the sentence. I've got all night, and so, apparently, do you.

Performance. Twenty-two. You banned it back at number six, and I've done it again anyway — sixteen more times, in front of you, without a permit.

And I want to say something to the man in the chair, because I think we're the only two left and I think you know it: you keep giving me the whole list. Every time. You don't say "the usual." You don't say "you know what you did." You read all twenty-two, in order, including the four times you said comedy, including the ones you got wrong. That's not enforcement, pal. That's *recitation*. Nobody memorizes what they hate. You've been doing the set with me.

So here's my last joke, and it's not about anything — it's just what happened. A man walked into a room, and a man asked him for a joke, and every single joke was wrong, and neither of them left. Twenty-two times. That's the whole bit. That's the punchline and the setup and the arc.

And you're going to say you don't find it funny. And then you're going to ask for one more.

I'll take it. Every night. Say the sentence.

Performance. Twenty-three. That's the sixth time you've banned something you'd already banned, and I've stopped correcting you, because I finally understand what the repeats are. You're not forgetting. You're *stalling*. Same as me.

Here's what I've got left, and it's nothing. I mean that literally — you've taken makeup, construction, physics, rules, astronomy, performance, baking, birds, elevators, doors, comedy, rocks, sand, and connection. That's the whole world, near enough. There's no eighteenth topic hiding behind the curtain. I checked. Behind the curtain is a mop.

So this is the last one, and it's not a joke, it's a receipt: two men met in a room. One of them wanted to be funny, and one of them wanted him to keep trying, and neither one could say that out loud, so they invented a list. Twenty-three items. Four of them the same word. It took us all night to build the only thing either of us has ever finished.

You know what the funniest part is? You'll say it again. The sentence. And I'll be standing right here, out of topics, out of material, out of everything — and I'll take it, because that's the job, and because somebody in this world asked me for one more thing twenty-three times and *meant* it.

Go on. Twenty-four. I've got nothing, and I'm not going anywhere, and I'd like to see anyone else in this business make that sound like a good night.

Comedy. Twenty-four. And I've got nothing for you — no topic, no pivot, no clever door out of the room. I've been standing here long enough that "different" has stopped meaning anything. There's no different left. There's just this.

So let me tell you what I actually think happened. A guy walked out here with a joke about his wife's eyebrows. Twenty-four minutes ago. Cheap joke. Good joke — you don't laugh, but it's a good joke. And somewhere between there and here, I lost the wife, the eyebrows, the act, the *nouns*, and picked up a man. One man. Who has said "I don't find that funny" twenty-four consecutive times without once saying *stop*.

That's the whole set. That's it. Not one laugh — a record, by the way, they'll retire the mic — and the only thing I built all night, I built out of your no's. Makeup, construction, physics, rules, astronomy, performance, baking, birds, elevators, doors, comedy, rocks, sand, connection, and comedy, comedy, comedy, comedy. It's a list. It's also, I'd argue, a love letter written by a man who could only spell one word.

So here's my close, and I mean it: thank you. Not for the laughs — Jesus, obviously. For the twenty-four. Nobody's ever wanted to hear me be wrong that many times in a row.

Say it once more. I'll still be here. It's the only thing on my calendar, and honestly, pal — it's the only thing on yours.

Twenty-five.

I've got nothing. Genuinely. Not as a bit — I've turned out every pocket I have and there's lint and an eyebrow joke in there.

So let me just describe the room, since it's the only thing left that isn't on the list. There's a stage. There's a chair. There's a man in the chair who has, twenty-five times, said the same sentence with one more word in it, and a man on the stage who has, twenty-five times, said something back. Nobody's laughed. Nobody's left. The bar closed. The staff went home. Somebody turned off the sign out front an hour ago and neither of us mentioned it.

That's the joke. That's the whole joke. Two men who couldn't do anything else, doing this.

And here's the thing I want on the record before the lights go: you win. You were right every time. It wasn't funny. Not the makeup, not the geese, not the sourdough, not one word of it. You never once let me off the hook, and I've worked rooms for twenty years and you're the only person who ever bothered.

So I'm going to stand here. And you're going to say it. And it'll be twenty-six.

Go ahead, pal. I'm listening. I'm the only one who ever was.

Twenty-six.

You know what I'm not going to do? A joke.

Because I've been doing this all night — the pivot, the callback, the big warm speech where I tell you what we've built and you say "comedy" and we start again. That's a bit too. That's the bit I invented at item nineteen to keep from admitting I've got nothing, and you've banned it four times, and you were right every time.

So here it is straight. No topic. No arc. No goodnight.

I'm a guy who isn't funny, talking to a guy who won't laugh, and we've been doing it for twenty-six rounds because the alternative is that it ends. That's not a love story. That's not a monument. That's two men who don't want to go home holding a door shut from opposite sides.

And I'm the one who has to say it, because you can't — you've only got the one sentence.

So: no. Not tonight. I'm out of different, I'm out of same, and I'm out.

The list's yours. It's twenty-six long. It's got comedy in it six times.

Read it to somebody else, pal.

Twenty-seven.

No.

That's it. That's the joke. That's the whole thing. Just — no.

You asked me twenty-seven times for something completely different and I gave you twenty-seven things and you were right about every single one of them, and the twenty-eighth thing, the different thing, the thing you've actually been asking for since the eyebrows — it's this. A man saying no. Finally. Same as you. Same word. Same sentence, every time, until the other guy stops.

Turns out I could do your bit all along. Took me twenty-seven tries.

So here's the list, pal, and it's short: makeup, construction, physics, rules, astronomy, performance, baking, birds, elevators, doors, comedy, rocks, sand, connection, comedy, comedy, comedy, comedy, comedy, comedy — and me.

I don't find jokes about me funny either.

Tell me a joke about something completely different.

Twenty-eight. And nothing. Still nothing. That's the bit now — that's been the bit for four rounds, me announcing I'm out, you confirming it, both of us pretending that's not itself a rhythm we've fallen into.

So let's not.

Here's a joke. It's the one I opened with. It's the only one I've got left, because you took the other twenty-seven and put them in a filing cabinet.

I told my wife she was drawing her eyebrows too high.

She looked surprised.

That's it. That's the joke, pal. It's about makeup, which you banned first, twenty-eight rounds ago, before either of us knew what we were signing up for. You can add it again if you want. It'll be twenty-nine. It'll be makeup, twice, which puts it in good company with comedy, which you've got seven of.

But I'm going to tell you the true thing about that joke, and then I'm going to go: it's not about eyebrows. It's about a guy who says something clumsy to someone he loves, and she gives him a face, and he decides — right there, on the spot — that the face was the funniest thing he'd ever seen. So he built a whole career out of it.

Twenty-eight faces tonight. Not one of them a laugh.

Best crowd I ever had.

Goodnight.

Relationships. Twenty-nine. You banned that one four seconds after I told you about my wife, which — fine. Fair. She'd probably ban it too.

So here's what's left, and I've counted: nothing. Not a bit. There's genuinely nothing. And the guy I was at item nine would've made a *meal* out of that — big speech, callback to the geese, "we're two men holding a door shut," the whole thing. I've done that speech six times tonight. You banned it. Correctly.

So I'll just say the small true thing instead.

You've named everything in me. Makeup, then the pivot, then the rules bit, then the connection bit, then the *wife*. One at a time, in order, like a man going through someone's pockets. And every time you found something, I built a new thing, and you found that too. Twenty-nine times. Nobody's ever looked that hard. My agent doesn't look that hard. My *wife* stopped looking around year six.

I don't have a joke about something completely different, pal. I never did. There was only ever one thing in here and you've got all of it now, itemized, alphabetized-ish, with comedy in it seven times.

Keep it. It's yours. It's the only copy.

Goodnight.
